name: barbell one leg squat
instructions: Stand with your feet shoulder-width apart and hold a barbell across your upper back.
Lift one foot off the ground and extend it forward, keeping it parallel to the ground.
Bend your standing leg and lower your body down as if sitting back into a chair, keeping your chest up and your back straight.
Lower yourself until your thigh is parallel to the ground, then push through your heel to return to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, then switch legs and repeat.

Stand with your feet shoulder-width apart and hold a barbell across your upper back. Lift one foot off the ground and extend it forward, keeping it parallel to the ground. Bend your standing leg and lower your body down as if sitting back into a chair, keeping your chest up and your back straight. Lower yourself until your thigh is parallel to the ground, then push through your heel to return to the starting position. Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, then switch legs and repeat.

conditionNotes.note: Axial loading under fatigue may worsen asymmetrical curves. Prefer split-stance or supported variations.
Use pain-free range of motion, slower tempo, and lighter progressions when joints feel irritated.
Loaded spinal flexion or heavy axial loading should be avoided. Use supported variations.
Maintain neutral spine and avoid positions that aggravate the lower back.
Maintain consistent breathing throughout. Avoid breath-holding.
Both aerobic and resistance training improve insulin sensitivity. Monitor blood sugar before, during, and after sessions. Have a fast-acting carb available.
Rhythm-based exercises and large-amplitude movements are beneficial. Focus on motor confidence.
Focus on muscle co-contraction and joint stability. Avoid locking out joints passively.
Functional patterns are generally safe. Prioritize pelvic floor awareness and avoid Valsalva.
Start light with higher reps and shorter ROM. Progress load gradually as pain-free capacity increases.
